video_title: Why Tomatoes Are the Most Expensive They've Been in Four Decades | Odd Lots
gist: Baldor's SVP of Procurement Jacob Krempel explains the 2026 tomato price spike (record ~$2.69/lb, +40% YoY) as a supply shock from two Florida freezes that wiped out ~80% of the FL crop plus a US anti-dumping tariff on Mexican tomatoes that cut Mexican supply ~3-4%. He details how inelastic produce demand amplifies weather shocks, how retailers ratchet prices up like a rocket and down like a feather while restaurants (informed buyers) do not, and broader cost pressures across freight, fertilizer, beef, and the structural shift of US tomato production to Mexico and to high-margin specialty/snacking varieties.

insights.claim: US tomato prices hit a record ~$2.69/lb, up ~40% year-over-year as of June 2026, driven primarily by weather rather than input costs.
insights.speaker: Tracy Alloway / Joe Weisenthal
insights.so_what: Confirms the spike is a transient supply shock, not a structural cost reset, implying mean reversion once supply recovers.
insights.quote: tomato prices are at a record $2.69 a pound

insights.claim: Two Florida freezes this winter wiped out ~80% of the Florida crop, removing roughly 26 of every 100 tomatoes the US winter market needs, and against inelastic demand this is what produced the price spike.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Quantifies how a localized weather event translates into a national price spike via inelastic demand—a template for other produce shocks.
insights.quote: there are two freezes that happened in Florida this winter that took, 80% of the crop in Florida out completely

insights.claim: Over $150 million worth of Florida tomatoes were lost to the freezes.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Sizes the supply destruction behind the spike for anyone modeling produce commodity risk.

insights.claim: Wholesale tomato prices have already normalized—average case price was ~$65 in May but by June 2026 had fallen back to the five-year average, even as retail prices stay elevated.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: The retail-wholesale divergence signals retailers capturing margin on the way down; wholesale buyers should expect relief that may not pass to consumers.
insights.quote: in April, much closer to the five year average to May was 65 bucks a case on average for the whole month

insights.claim: Produce demand in the US is highly inelastic in the short term and heavily dependent on weather, acreage and yield, producing wild and frequent price swings across crops.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Frames produce as a structurally volatile commodity class where small supply changes cause large price moves—useful for anticipating the next spike.
insights.quote: produce demand, especially in the US, is rather inelastic in the short term

insights.claim: The tomato spike is part of a rolling sequence of produce spikes—romaine is already near $100 a case on bad California weather and avocados jumped from $30 to $80 a case in a single week.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Signals continued food-inflation headlines rotating across crops; watch romaine and avocados as the next consumer-price stories.
insights.quote: I guarantee this summer you're going to be hearing about romaine again. Because that's already almost $100 a case right now

themes.theme: Tariffs and the Mexico-Florida Tomato Trade

insights.claim: About 70% of US winter tomato production now comes from Mexico and 30% from the US, a complete reversal from the immediate post-NAFTA ~80% domestic / 20% Mexico split.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: US winter tomato supply is structurally dependent on Mexico, making it acutely sensitive to trade policy and the peso.
insights.quote: About 70% of our tomato production in the winter is done in Mexico now

insights.claim: A ~17% US anti-dumping tariff on Mexican tomatoes implemented in July 2025 (replacing prior suspension agreements) cut Mexican production ~3-4% this year and created more price uncertainty than the old price floors.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Trade policy compounded the weather shock; the anti-dumping case is an ongoing structural risk to US tomato supply and price.
insights.quote: That changed, last year in July when they, the U.S. government reopened the case and put the anti-dumping tariff on Mexico

insights.claim: Because Mexico supplies ~70% of the US winter market, even a 3-4% Mexican production decline removes meaningful volume—amplified because it coincided with the Florida freeze.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: A 'perfect storm' of simultaneous supply hits is how produce spikes turn extreme; correlated supply shocks are the key risk to monitor.
insights.quote: we did see Mexico decline their production a little bit this year. Probably 3 to 4% doesn't sound like a lot, but when they're supplying 70% of the U.S. market in the winter, that was actually quite a bit

insights.claim: Mexican growers achieve 3-4x more tomato yield per acre using shade houses (netting) and greenhouses versus open-field growing, having imported greenhouse technology from Canada's Leamington region.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Explains Mexico's durable cost/yield advantage that underpins its dominance and makes a domestic reshoring of tomato supply hard.
insights.quote: with a shade house in Mexico, you get almost 3 to 4 extra return per acre on the amount of tomatoes that you can produce versus if you just left it out in the sun

insights.claim: Retailers raise produce prices 'like a rocket' and lower them 'like a feather,' using high-price headlines to establish a higher price floor that consumers accept even after wholesale costs fall.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Asymmetric price stickiness at retail is a real margin lever, not a myth—relevant to grocer margin durability during disinflation.
insights.quote: retailers, they go they take their prices up like a rocket and down like a feather

insights.claim: Restaurant and chef buyers are informationally sophisticated (checking USDA prices and field projections) and cannot be 'fooled' on direction, so the rocket-up/feather-down dynamic does not work in food service—food service is 'up like a rocket, down like a rock.'
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Pricing power asymmetry exists only versus uninformed retail consumers; the 'informed buyer' is the constraint on extracting margin.
insights.quote: We're up like a rocket, down like a rock, because our, chefs, and, and some of the buying teams that they have are much more knowledgeable about markets than the average consumer

insights.claim: The internet's promise of an informed consumer was offset by retailers becoming equally well-informed about customers and deploying counter-tactics, so the information edge net accrued to sellers.
insights.speaker: Joe Weisenthal (host)
insights.so_what: A general framework for why digitization did not commoditize consumer-facing pricing as predicted.
insights.quote: the part that they didn't talk about was that the retailer would be very well informed about the customer, and that they would be just as savvy and have all kinds of counter tactics

insights.claim: The beef retail-to-wholesale spread is at a record high—roughly 40-45% versus ~30% twenty years ago—even as beef demand stays strong at extreme prices (e.g. ~$140 strip steaks on menus).
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Widening cutout spread is another sign of retailer margin capture and inelastic protein demand; bullish for grocer/protein retail margins.
insights.quote: go back 20 years. The difference between the price per pound of beef at wholesale and retail is probably 30%. Now it's probably closer to 40 or 45%

insights.claim: Grocery EBITDA margins have stayed roughly stable at ~4-7% over 15 years because higher costs (delivery, click-and-collect, tech, warehouses, more SKUs/shrink) are offset by new revenue from selling customer purchasing data to suppliers.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Grocers are now part advertising/data businesses; retail-media monetization is what preserves margins amid e-commerce cost drag.
insights.quote: they're much more advertising and marketing companies now where they're, they're selling customer data to their suppliers

insights.claim: Grocery produce departments have expanded from 75-100 SKUs in the 1970s to 300-500 SKUs today, adding variety but also shrink and handling cost.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: SKU proliferation is a structural cost driver behind grocery's higher cost base and a reason prices stay elevated.
insights.quote: Grocery store today is 300 to 500 SKUs in their produce department

insights.claim: Food is only ~30% of a restaurant's total cost (vs ~70-75% for a grocer), so restaurants can tolerate volatile ingredient prices and prefer daily/weekly pricing to shop around, while grocers care more about locking long-duration contracts.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Explains who hedges and who doesn't in the food supply chain; menu prices are driven as much by labor/rent/utilities as by food.
insights.quote: the cost of food is about 30% of their overall cost

insights.claim: A NYC burger that was ~$14-15 in 2019 is now ~$22-23, but the food cost rose only ~$2 (from ~$3.50 to ~$5.50)—most of the menu price increase reflects labor, rent and utility inflation, not ingredients.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Menu inflation is mostly non-food operating cost—counters the assumption that ingredient prices drive restaurant pricing.
insights.quote: let's take a burger in New York 2019, it was probably about 14, 15 bucks on the menu today. A good burger on the menu is is 22 or $23

themes.theme: Logistics, Freight and Fertilizer Cost Pressures

insights.claim: Team-driver truck rates from Salinas, California to the East Coast jumped from ~$10-11k eight months ago to as high as $16-17k weeks ago—the highest Krempel has seen in his career.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: A ~50-60% freight cost surge feeds directly into produce prices; freight is a live inflation driver for perishables.
insights.quote: Eight months ago might have been ten, $11,000 for that. We we saw prices a couple weeks ago as high as 16, $17,000, which I, I have never seen, in my whole career

insights.claim: Trucking costs are surging from rising oil prices plus a shrinking truck supply, partly because immigration crackdowns are reducing Mexican cross-border truckers.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Immigration policy is an under-appreciated supply-side driver of freight inflation and thus food prices.
insights.quote: some of the crackdown on immigration, sort of like truckers that are crossing the border from Mexico and concerns there, we are seeing the the supply of trucks shrink

insights.claim: Higher fertilizer costs will feed into retail tomato prices over the next ~6 months because growers' currently pre-bought/locked fertilizer is set to roll off, and growers are already flagging cost increases for next year's contracts.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: A delayed, telegraphed cost-push to produce prices in H2 2026 even after the weather shock passes—watch fertilizer pass-through.
insights.quote: we're going to see over the next six months, is is that's going to start to evaporate

insights.claim: Cold-chain complexity is a structural cost: different products require different temperatures (berries ~32-34F, tomatoes/onions ~48-52F) on the same truck, and serving NYC/Hunts Point is more expensive than other US markets.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Cold-chain and dense-market delivery are persistent cost layers that keep fresh-produce margins thin and prices high in major metros.

themes.theme: Grower Margins and the Specialty Tomato Shift

insights.claim: Tomato growing is a razor-thin, single-digit-margin business; growers typically contract ~60-70% of a crop for guaranteed return and sell the remaining ~30% on the open USDA market to catch upswings.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Even during price spikes, growers who lost crop do not profit—the spike accrues downstream; grower economics stay fragile.
insights.quote: this is a razor thin margin business, just like ours is as well

insights.claim: Growers are shifting acreage toward higher-flavor 'snacking' tomato varieties and away from older/blander ones, driven by consumer demand for tomatoes eaten as snacks rather than only in salads.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: A demand-driven product-mix shift toward premium varieties is reshaping tomato supply and pricing tiers.

insights.claim: Round and Roma tomatoes are substitutable so their prices move together; when Florida rounds were wiped out, pressure spilled into Romas and then into cherry/vine-ripe varieties as buyers scrambled for any tomato.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Cross-variety substitution means a shock in one tomato type propagates across the whole category—correlation rises in scarcity.

insights.claim: Fresh and canned tomato prices are uncorrelated—canned/processing tomatoes are mostly grown in California with different varieties and long-term contracts, giving processors tighter cost control than the fresh market.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: A fresh-tomato spike does not imply canned-tomato inflation—useful for separating CPG input exposure from fresh-produce shocks.
insights.quote: They're completely different markets

insights.claim: New high-flavor tomato varieties are created via multi-year crossbreeding at the seed-company level (e.g. Sunset/Mastronardi R&D), not genetic engineering, with growers iterating on flavor/yield feedback—an innovation pipeline that originated in Holland.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Value creation in produce is migrating upstream to seed genetics and branding; specialty varieties are a differentiated, defensible product layer.
insights.quote: it's almost all crossbreeding and it's, it's done at the, the mostly at the seed company level

insights.claim: Demand for new tomato varieties flows top-down from restaurants to retail to consumers (chefs feature a variety, diners then want it at home), amplified heavily by social media and Instagram-driven food trends.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Restaurants and social media are the demand-creation engine for premium produce—brand and storytelling, not price, drive specialty adoption.

insights.claim: Greenhouse snacking-tomato production has faced new viruses sweeping through traditional greenhouse varieties, forcing growers to tear out plants and soil and replant, which had already slightly reduced acreage/production before the freeze.
insights.speaker: Jacob Krempel (Baldor SVP Procurement & Merchandising)
insights.so_what: Disease is a pre-existing supply drag layered under the weather shock; biological risk keeps specialty tomato supply volatile.
